codex.entry.core_workflow.content:
Forge Studio\u2019s workflow is a loop: describe \u2192 generate \u2192 paint \u2192 iterate.

Type what you want to see in the prompt box, hit Generate, and the AI creates an image on your canvas. From there, you can paint on top of it, then generate again \u2014 the AI works with whatever is already on the canvas.

You\u2019re building images with your hands and the AI together. Every generation can be a starting point, a refinement, or a complete reimagining.
codex.entry.core_workflow.deep: Studio auto-routes based on canvas state: blank canvas \u2192 txt2img, canvas with content \u2192 img2img, mask painted \u2192 inpainting. The function isCanvasBlank() is the sole source of truth for this routing \u2014 it checks actual pixel data, not a dirty flag. Denoise strength controls how much the AI changes existing content (1.0 = full replace, 0.3 = gentle refinement).
codex.entry.tutorials_and_tours.content:
Studio has interactive tutorials that walk you through features hands-on, in the actual UI, on a real canvas. Three places to find them.

1. Show Me buttons in Codex. Many entries in this Codex have a blue Show Me button at the top. Click it and Studio opens a new tutorial document, sets the scene up for you, and walks you through the feature step-by-step. Currently available for: Inpainting, Regional Prompting, ControlNet, Layers, LoRA Browser, Brush Dynamics, Wildcards, Symmetry Painting, and the Core Workflow.

2. The first-run path picker. The first time you launched Studio, you saw a card with two options:

\u2022 I\u2019m new to AI image generation \u2014 progressive walkthrough that makes your first image, then reveals controls in groups as you go. Branches to a model-acquisition flow if Studio detects you don\u2019t have any models installed yet, and to an inpaint flow if you click Result \u2192 Canvas. Persists \u2014 if you close mid-walkthrough, it resumes where you left off.

\u2022 I\u2019ve used Stable Diffusion, Photoshop, or similar tools \u2014 spatial orientation tour. Full UI visible from the start, focuses on what\u2019s different about Studio compared to other AI/art tools.

You can also skip and figure things out yourself.

3. Restart from Settings. Open the Settings tab in the right panel and find the Education section. Two buttons:

\u2022 Restart Tour \u2014 replays your current path from the beginning.

\u2022 Change Path \u2014 brings the first-run picker back so you can switch between Beginner and Experienced.

None of these block you. You can dismiss any tour with Escape, and Show Me tutorials happen on a separate document so they never disturb your real work.
codex.entry.first_image.content:
1. Type a description in the prompt box \u2014 \"a cat on a mountain at sunset\" works fine.

2. Click Generate (or Ctrl+Enter).

3. Wait a few seconds. Your image appears in the output gallery.

4. Click Result \u2192 Canvas to place it on the canvas for editing.

Every image is unique \u2014 same prompt, different seed, different result. Try generating a few times to see the variety.
codex.entry.ui_layout.content:
Left: Toolstrip \u2014 your painting tools (brush, eraser, fill, and 16 more).

Center: Canvas \u2014 scroll to zoom, Space+drag to pan. This is where your image lives.

Right: Panel \u2014 three tabs: Generate (prompt, settings, output), Extensions (ControlNet, ADetailer), and Settings. \\ collapses it for more canvas space.

Top: Context bar \u2014 shows controls for whichever tool is active (size, opacity, hardness, etc.).

Bottom: Status bar \u2014 generation progress, VRAM usage, canvas dimensions.

Tab bar: Switch between Studio (canvas), Develop, Gallery, Workshop, Wildcards, and Codex (you\u2019re here).
codex.entry.models_and_vae.content:
The model (also called a checkpoint) is the AI brain that generates your images. Without one, Studio can\u2019t generate anything. Different models produce completely different styles \u2014 photorealistic, anime, painterly, abstract.

Getting your first model:

1. Download a model file. Here\u2019s a good one to start with: ToxicHost\u2019s recommended starter model

2. Put the downloaded file in your Forge folder: models/Stable-diffusion/

3. In Studio, click the refresh button (\u21bb) next to the Model dropdown. Your model appears \u2014 no restart needed.

4. Select it from the dropdown. Make sure the architecture selector (radio buttons: SD 1.5 / XL / Flux) matches your model \u2014 wrong architecture = errors.

Find more models on CivitAI (checkpoints, LoRAs, community content) and Hugging Face (official releases).

Architectures:

\u2022 SD 1.5 \u2014 smaller, faster, huge ecosystem. ~4 GB VRAM.

\u2022 SDXL / Illustrious \u2014 higher quality, more VRAM (6\u20137 GB). Illustrious models excel at anime and illustration styles.

\u2022 Flux \u2014 newer architecture with different prompt handling. Needs separate text encoders.

SD1.5, SDXL, and Flux checkpoints, LoRAs, and ControlNet models are not cross-compatible.

Model formats:

\u2022 .safetensors \u2014 standard, always prefer this.

\u2022 .gguf \u2014 quantized, uses less VRAM at some quality cost. Good for larger models on smaller GPUs.

\u2022 fp16 / fp8 \u2014 numerical precision. Lower = less VRAM, slightly less quality. fp16 is the sweet spot for most. Set \"Diffusion in Low Bits\" to \"Automatic\" for fp8/GGUF models.

VAE: The VAE handles color processing. Leave it on \"Automatic\" \u2014 most models include their own. If your colors look washed out or oversaturated, try switching to a specific VAE from the dropdown.
codex.entry.models_and_vae.deep: Model dropdown pulls from Forge\u2019s checkpoint directory. Hot-reload via refresh button \u2014 no restart. VAE \"Automatic\" uses baked-in or Forge global. For SD1.5 color issues try vae-ft-mse-840000. SDXL uses dual text encoders (CLIP-L + CLIP-G). Flux may require separate text encoder downloads. Some models need keywords in their path to be recognized (e.g. Flux-Kontext needs \"kontext\" in the filename).
codex.entry.output_workflow.content:
Generated images appear in the output gallery strip at the bottom of the Generate panel.

Result \u2192 Canvas places the image on your canvas as a new layer \u2014 it doesn\u2019t overwrite your existing work. Pipeline results (generation, upscale) go to a reusable \"Gen Result\" layer.

Save writes to disk (auto-save is on by default). You can change the format in Settings: PNG, JPEG, or WebP. Embed metadata stores your prompt and settings inside saved files so other tools (PNG Info, other AI UIs) can read them later.

Gallery always remembers: Studio\u2019s Gallery tracks generation params for every image regardless of the Embed metadata setting. It uses a pixel fingerprint, so metadata survives file renames, moves between folders, and format conversions (PNG \u2192 JPG \u2192 WebP). Turning off Embed metadata only affects whether other tools can read it from the file itself.

Filename convention: Auto-saved files use Studio-NNNNN-seed.ext, where NNNNN is a 5-digit counter per output folder and seed is the exact seed used for that image. Batched images get sequential counters and sequential seeds.

Workflow defaults: Save your current parameter settings (sampler, scheduler, CFG, steps, denoise, and more) as defaults via Settings \u2192 Defaults \u2192 Save Current as Defaults. These persist server-side and load automatically on next launch. Separate from per-tool brush settings, which are client-side.
codex.entry.output_workflow.deep:
Gallery metadata is keyed by SHA256 of the decoded RGB pixel data, computed locally in compute_content_hash(). Generation writes the metadata row to the Gallery DB immediately via save_metadata_by_hash(), without waiting for a Gallery scan. When the scan later finds the file on disk, it links the existing hash-keyed metadata row to the filepath-keyed image row. Because the key is the pixel fingerprint, not the path, operations that preserve pixel identity (rename, move, lossless format conversion) keep the metadata attached. Lossy re-encoding will break the link \u2014 the new file has different pixels, so it gets a new hash.

Filename counter is determined per-folder at save time by scanning for existing Studio-NNNNN-* files and incrementing the highest match, so manual deletions in a folder don\u2019t create gaps that get filled later.
codex.entry.color_picker.content:
Click the toolstrip swatches to open the color picker. HSV wheel + hex input for precise colors.

X swaps foreground and background colors. D resets them to black and white.

Ctrl+Click on the canvas samples a color from any tool without switching away. I switches to the dedicated eyedropper tool.

Studio keeps a rolling history of your last 10 colors as clickable swatches below the picker.
codex.entry.shortcuts_tools.content:
B Brush • E Eraser • I Eyedropper • G Fill/Gradient • U Shape • T Text

S Smudge • R Blur • J Dodge/Burn • K Clone • Y Liquify • P Pixelate

M Rect select • O Ellipse select • L Lasso • W Wand • C Crop • V Transform

Q Toggle mask mode • D Reset colors • X Swap FG/BG
codex.entry.shortcuts_canvas.content:
Ctrl+Z Undo • Ctrl+Shift+Z Redo

Ctrl+A Select all • Ctrl+D Deselect • Ctrl+Shift+I Invert selection

Ctrl+C/X/V Copy/Cut/Paste

Del / Backspace — context-sensitive:

• Active transform → cancel transform, discard its contents.

• Active selection → clear pixels inside the selection.

• No selection, no transform → resets the canvas — removes all layers and creates a fresh white Background. Undoable with Ctrl+Z.

Ctrl+Enter Generate • \ Toggle panel

Ctrl+] / Ctrl+[ Cycle documents

F or 0 Zoom to fit • Scroll Zoom • Space+Drag Pan

[/] Brush size • {/} Brush hardness • Shift+Drag Size + opacity adjust

Ctrl+Click Eyedropper from any tool • Alt+Click Clone source

Ctrl+Shift+N New layer • Ctrl+J Duplicate layer • Ctrl+E Merge down • Ctrl+Shift+E Flatten

Enter Commit transform • Escape Cancel / deselect
codex.entry.layers_basics.content:
Layers are transparent sheets stacked on each other. + adds a new layer, ▲▼ reorder, ⇩ merge down, eye toggles visibility. Each layer has its own Opacity and Blend Mode.

Generated images land on the reference layer (bottom). Your painting goes on paint layers above. This means regenerating never destroys your painted edits — they live on a different layer.

Blend modes (Normal, Multiply, Screen, Overlay, Soft Light, Hard Light, Difference, etc.) control how layers interact. Merge Down composites using the current blend mode and opacity.
codex.entry.psd_export.content:
Export your full layer stack as a PSD file that opens in Photoshop, Krita, GIMP, or any PSD-compatible editor. Click Export in the Layers section.

Layer names, blend modes, opacity, and visibility are all preserved. You can continue working in your preferred editor without losing your stack.
codex.entry.psd_export.deep: Built via ag-psd.js. Served via dedicated FastAPI route. Firefox uses POST + download (no showSaveFilePicker support).
codex.entry.inpaint_basics.content:
Inpainting lets you selectively change parts of an image while keeping the rest untouched. It’s one of the most powerful and commonly used features in Studio.

Step by step:

1. Have an image on your canvas (generate one or load one).

2. Press Q or click Mask. Your brush now paints a red overlay instead of color.

3. Paint red over the area you want to change.

4. Write a prompt describing what should appear in the masked area.

5. Hit Generate. Only the red area changes.

6. Press Q again to exit mask mode.

Whole Picture vs Only Masked:

Whole Picture generates the full canvas at your set resolution. The AI sees everything and the result blends naturally. Use for: removing objects, changing backgrounds, anything where context matters.

Only Masked crops just the masked region, scales it up, generates, then composites back. Much more detail for small areas. Use for: fixing faces, refining details. Increase the Padding slider so the AI sees context around the mask.

Fill mode — what goes under the mask:

• Original — keeps existing pixels as the starting point. Best for most tasks.

• Fill — replaces with solid average color. Best for removing objects — the AI fills naturally instead of building on top.

• Latent Noise / Latent Nothing — more creative freedom, less predictable.

Denoise guide:

• 0.3–0.5 — subtle refinement, artifact cleanup.

• 0.5–0.7 — moderate reshaping.

• 0.7–0.9 — major replacement.

• 1.0 — complete replacement, nothing preserved.

Blur: Softens the mask edge. Higher values (8–16) = smoother transitions. Use 0 for hard cuts.

Common recipes:

• Fix a face: Only Masked + Original + Padding 64–128 + Denoise 0.4–0.6.

• Remove an object: Whole Picture + Fill + Denoise 0.7–0.9. Describe replacement.

• Add something new: Original + Denoise 0.7–0.9. Describe the addition.

• Outpaint: Resize canvas larger, mask empty area, Fill + Denoise 0.8–1.0.
codex.entry.inpaint_basics.deep:
Mask is a separate canvas buffer, not a layer. "Only Masked" with padding = A1111’s "Inpaint at full resolution" — crops masked region plus padding, scales to gen resolution, generates, composites back with mask feathering.

Soft Inpainting (toggle visible when mask mode active): modifies the pipeline for smooth boundary blending. Parameters: Schedule Bias, Preservation, Transition Contrast, Mask Influence, Difference Threshold, Difference Contrast. Best with feathered masks. Adds processing overhead.
codex.entry.regional_prompting.content:
Regional prompting lets you assign different descriptions to different areas of your canvas. Instead of one prompt for the whole image, you can say "forest on the left, city on the right."

Step by step:

1. In the Generate tab, scroll to Regions and click + to add a region.

2. A new region appears with a color swatch and prompt field.

3. Switch to the brush (B) and paint on the canvas. The region’s color marks where it applies.

4. Type a prompt for this region — describe what should appear in the painted area.

5. Add more regions as needed. Each gets its own color and prompt.

6. Your main prompt still affects the whole image. Regions add local control on top.

7. Hit Generate.

Tips:

• Regions can overlap. Where they do, both prompts influence.

• Small regions work. Studio’s system is specifically designed for this — small painted regions get their own content regardless of how much canvas area larger regions occupy.

• Leave unpainted gaps between regions. Those gaps act as coherence bridges — lighting, perspective, and style flow through the background. Painting regions edge-to-edge with no gap tends to produce harder seams.

• Use the main prompt for overall qualities (lighting, style) and regions for specific content. The main prompt is automatically prepended to each region.

• Don’t forget to clear regions when done — they persist until removed.

For the technical architecture, see the Attention Couple entry below.
codex.entry.attention_couple.content:
Attention Couple is the name of the system that makes regional prompting work in Studio. You don’t interact with it directly — you paint regions and it handles the rest. This entry is for the curious.

What it does: When you paint multiple regions with different prompts, Attention Couple steers the model’s internal attention so each region’s content ends up where you painted it, without bleeding into the other regions.

Why it’s different from other regional systems: Approaches like Forge Couple, Regional Prompter, and ComfyUI node-based solutions generate separate outputs and blend them together, or bias the attention outputs after the fact. Studio biases the attention itself, before the softmax that decides what each pixel pays attention to. That means small painted regions actually get their own content instead of being washed out by larger neighbors.

Why unpainted gaps matter: Studio also applies regional isolation on self-attention (pixel-to-pixel feature sharing). Without it, features leak laterally between subjects and you get heterochromia, hair color bleed, and other attribute mixing. Unpainted areas act as a shared background that every region can attend to, which is what lets perspective and lighting flow through the whole image coherently.
codex.entry.attention_couple.deep:
v7 architecture — dual-control pre-softmax biasing:

• attn2_patch + attn2_replace: cross-attention (text → spatial) pre-softmax logit bias. All region conditionings concatenated into one K tensor; Q @ Kᵀ computed manually with a spatial bias matrix added before softmax. Bias scale tied to region area via 1/sqrt(frac), capped at 12.

• attn1_replace: self-attention (pixel → pixel) regional isolation bias. Same-region positions attend freely; cross-region attention is negatively biased (−8.0); background ↔ anything attends freely. This is the coherence bridge.

• Uncond pass strips region tokens — if uncond attends to region content without bias, CFG cancels regional separation.

• 1−t² decay curve holds spatial control through the detail refinement phase.

• Global prompt is prepended to each region encoding. Gaussian feathering + bilinear downsampling on the region masks.

Inspired by Bounded Attention (Dahary et al., ECCV 2024) which proved cross-attention bias alone cannot prevent attribute leakage. First implementation of combined cross+self attention masking for regional prompting in the Forge/A1111 ecosystem. AD integration: each region carries ad_prompt (character-only, post-wildcard-resolution) so ADetailer inpaints single faces with clean per-character descriptions rather than the scene-level global prompt.
codex.entry.controlnet.content:
ControlNet gives the AI structural guidance — "follow this pose," "use this edge map," "keep this depth layout." It analyzes a source image and uses that structure to guide generation.

Common use cases:

Keep a pose: Preprocessor → OpenPose, Model → OpenPose ControlNet. The AI follows the detected pose but creates new content.

Keep edges/lines: Preprocessor → Canny, Model → Canny ControlNet. Maintains architectural layouts, mechanical designs, linework.

Keep depth layout: Preprocessor → Depth, Model → Depth ControlNet. Foreground/background relationships stay the same.

Key settings:

• Weight (0.0–2.0): How strongly the structure guide affects the result. 1.0 = full.

• Source: Canvas (composite), Active Layer (just selected layer), or Upload (external image).

• Start/End: Which portion of denoising ControlNet is active. 0.0–0.5 gives structural guidance early without constraining fine details later.

Studio has 2 ControlNet units — combine them (e.g., pose + depth).

Important: Preprocessor and model must match in type AND architecture (SD1.5 vs SDXL).
codex.entry.common_problems.content:
Black image: Model didn’t load or VRAM ran out. Check status bar — reload model from dropdown, reduce dimensions, or disable HiRes Fix.

Garbled/noisy result: CFG too high or wrong model for your settings. Lower CFG to 5–7. SDXL models need at least 768×768.

Generation is very slow: Steps + HiRes Fix + ADetailer are multiplicative. Each adds a full pass. Disable what you don’t need.

Model takes forever to load: The first time you load a model, Forge may need to download additional components (text encoders, VAE, safety checker files). This is a one-time download per model architecture. Subsequent loads are fast.

Same image every time: Your seed is locked. Check the Seed field — if it’s not -1, click the dice button to re-enable random seeds.

Wrong colors / washed out: Try a different VAE in the dropdown.

"Disconnected" in status bar: WebSocket dropped. Refresh the page. If persistent, check the Forge terminal for errors.

Extra fingers / bad anatomy: Common AI artifact. Use ADetailer for faces. Add "bad hands, extra fingers" to negatives. Inpaint specific problem areas manually.

LoRA has no effect: Architecture mismatch (SD1.5 LoRA on SDXL model), missing trigger word, or weight too low.

ControlNet error on RTX 5000 series: Some preprocessors (especially Depth Anything) crash due to xformers not supporting CUDA capability 12.0. Use Canny or other preprocessors that don’t rely on xformers.

Inpainting fails on Mac (MPS): aten::hash_tensor.out is not implemented for MPS in PyTorch 2.10+. Set the environment variable PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1 before launching Forge. Inpainting will route to CPU for the unsupported op.
codex.entry.vram_management.content:
VRAM is your GPU’s memory. The loaded model is the biggest user (4–8 GB). Status bar shows current usage, color-coded: green (<60%), amber (60–85%), red (>85%).

Running low? Reduce image dimensions (768 instead of 1024), keep Batch Size at 1, disable HiRes Fix and ControlNet when not in use.

Unloading: Settings → VRAM → Unload Model frees all VRAM. Auto-unload does this after an idle timeout (configurable 5–30 minutes). The model reloads on next generation. Feature suggested by SnekySnek.

GGUF notes: On 16 GB cards, avoid --reserve-vram 2 with GGUF checkpoints — it forces full model offloading and tanks performance.
codex.entry.vram_management.deep: VRAM monitoring via /studio/vram. Manual unload via /studio/unload_model. Auto-unload fires via WebSocket after configurable idle timeout. SDXL ~6–7 GB, SD1.5 ~4 GB, ControlNet +1–2 GB per loaded unit, HiRes 2× = 4× latent tensor during second pass.
codex.entry.low_vram_optimization.conten:
Running SDXL on 6-8 GB cards can feel painfully slow if Forge's memory manager is being forced into aggressive offloading. Symptoms: base generation is fine but upscale takes 5+ minutes, hires denoise runs at 20+ s/it instead of 1-3 s/it, system RAM hits 85%+ during upscale, SSD activity spikes during generation.

Launch args to avoid on low-VRAM cards:

• --reserve-vram 1.5 (or any value above 1.0) — forces partial model offload on every sampling step. Modern Forge Neo's adaptive memory manager handles 6GB SDXL without manual reserve; the flag actively hurts performance.

• --cuda-stream on RTX 2060 / GTX 1080 / GTX 1660 — Forge's creator documents this produces NaN/black outputs on Turing cards. Use the Forge_Studio_LowVRAM.bat launcher (which doesn't carry this flag) instead of the regular one.

Forge Settings to enable:

• Settings → Upscaling → Composite the Tiles on GPU. Off by default. Moves tile stitching from CPU to GPU, cutting upscale time from ~17s to ~4s on typical setups.

• Settings → Upscaling → Prefer to load Upscaler in half precision. Halves the upscaler model's VRAM footprint. Requires a restart.

Resolution guidance for 6 GB cards:

• Safe: 768×768 base + 1.5x upscale + refine + ADetailer. Full pipeline runs in ~90 seconds after the fixes above.

• Sweet spot: 896×896 base + 1.5x upscale + full pipeline. Usually fits if you're not running other VRAM-heavy apps.

• Tight: 1024×1024 base — base gen alone is fine, but 1.5x upscale to 1536² often pushes refine into offload territory. Drop upscale to 1.25x, or skip refine, or stick to 896.

Studio VRAM Reserve slider: Settings → VRAM → VRAM Reserve. Leave at Auto (0). Manually setting a reserve on a 6GB card usually makes things slower, not faster, because it shrinks the model-weights budget. Only increase it if you're hitting actual OOM crashes — start with 0.8 GB, not higher.
codex.entry.low_vram_optimization.deep:
Root cause: Forge's built-in memory manager reserves ~1 GB of VRAM for inference workspace by default (minimum_inference_memory). Adding --reserve-vram 1.5 stacks another 1.5 GB on top, leaving ~3.5 GB of a 6 GB card for model weights. Since SDXL's UNet is ~5 GB, this forces 1.5+ GB of model to live in system RAM and get shuttled to VRAM on every forward pass. PCIe bandwidth becomes the bottleneck instead of GPU compute, producing the 10x slowdown symptom.

On 16 GB+ cards this doesn't happen because there's plenty of room for both the full model and workspace, so the flag has no measurable effect. It's specifically a low-VRAM footgun.
codex.entry.tool_brush.content:
Your primary painting tool. Adjust Size, Opacity, and Hardness in the context bar. Hardness controls edge softness — 100% is a sharp circle, lower values give a softer, airbrushed look. Smoothing stabilizes your strokes for cleaner curves. [/] to resize quickly.

Five presets, each suited to different tasks:

• Round — general purpose, clean edges. Your default for most painting.

• Flat — wide strokes with angle sensitivity. Good for broad fills and gradients.

• Scatter — textured spray of particles. Use for foliage, gravel, stars, or organic noise.

• Marker — firm edge with slight texture. Good for linework and outlines.

• Custom — load your own grayscale brush tip image.

Pressure sensitivity: Pen pressure toggles with S (pressure → size) and O (pressure → opacity). The Brush Dynamics panel (gear icon) exposes spacing, size jitter, opacity jitter, scatter, and rotation jitter.
codex.entry.tool_brush.deep: The opacity model uses a wet buffer — stamps draw at full opacity into a per-stroke buffer, and the completed stroke composites at the opacity ceiling. This prevents accumulation artifacts within a single stroke, matching Photoshop’s behavior. Brush dynamics include: spacing (fraction of diameter between stamps), size jitter, opacity jitter, scatter (random offset), rotation jitter, and follow-stroke rotation. Elliptical ratio and stipple/spike tips use Gaussian falloff via erf().
codex.entry.tool_eraser.content: Removes paint, making pixels transparent. On the background/reference layer, fills with white instead. Same controls and presets as the brush — size, opacity, hardness, dynamics all work the same way.
codex.entry.tool_eyedropper.content:
Click to sample a color from the canvas. Shortcut: Ctrl+Click from any brush tool samples without switching away.

Supports configurable radius averaging — instead of picking a single pixel, it averages an area around the sample point. Useful for getting a representative color from textured or noisy AI-generated regions.
codex.entry.tools_touchup.content:
Four brush-style tools for refining what’s already on the canvas. They all share the brush’s size and hardness controls; each adds its own Strength slider to control how aggressively it applies per stroke.

Smudge S — blends colors like wet paint. Drag to pull color in the direction of your stroke. Best for softening AI-generated edges that look too sharp — skin boundaries, hair edges, clothing folds. Strength around 30–50% gives the most controllable results.

Blur R — paints softness in place. Good for smoothing skin, softening backgrounds, or reducing noise in specific areas without affecting the whole image. Unlike Smudge, it doesn’t move pixels — it only blurs them where you paint.

Dodge / Burn J — Dodge lightens, Burn darkens. Toggle between them in the context bar. Adjusts lighting without changing the underlying colors — great for adding depth, shadows, highlights, or correcting uneven lighting in generated images.

Pixelate P — paints a pixelated/mosaic effect into the brushed area. Pixel block size scales with brush size; Strength controls how strong the pixelation is per stroke (lower values blend partially with the original). Useful for censoring faces, license plates, or text in generated images, or for stylized 8-bit aesthetic edits.

All four tools work with selections — if a selection is active, edits are constrained to it. They also remember their settings independently per tool (see Per-Tool Settings Memory).
codex.entry.tool_clone.content:
Copies pixels from one part of the canvas to another. Here’s how to use it:

1. Hold Alt and click on a clean area near the thing you want to remove. This sets the source point.

2. Now paint over the unwanted object. The tool copies pixels from the source area as you paint, covering the object with matching content.

3. The source point follows your brush — it moves relative to where you paint. If the clone starts picking up wrong content, Alt+click a new source.

Works great for removing watermarks, erasing unwanted objects, duplicating elements, or patching artifacts in generated images.
codex.entry.tool_liquify.content:
Pushes and warps pixels. Drag to distort the image in the direction you move. Great for adjusting proportions and fixing AI anatomy issues.

Use a large brush for broad adjustments (shoulder width, head tilt), small brush for fine-tuning (eye positions, jawlines, hand proportions). The distortion applies on stroke end.
codex.entry.tools_drawing.content:
Fill G — flood-fills an area with the foreground color.

Gradient G again — draws a smooth transition from foreground to background color. Linear or radial.

Shape U — rectangle, ellipse, or line. Fill or outline mode. Hold Shift for perfect squares/circles.

Text T — click to place, choose font and size. Renders to pixels on commit.
codex.entry.tools_selection.content:
Create selections to constrain painting, generation, or clipboard operations to a specific area.

• M Rectangular — click and drag a box.

• O Elliptical — click and drag an oval. Shift constrains to circle.

• L Lasso — freehand selection. Three modes via context bar subtool buttons: freehand lasso, polygon lasso (click-to-click straight segments), and magnetic lasso (snaps to edges).

• W Magic Wand — selects similar-colored areas. Strength controls tolerance.

Ctrl+D deselect • Ctrl+A select all • Ctrl+Shift+I invert • Ctrl+C/X/V clipboard • Delete clear selection.
codex.entry.tools_selection.deep: The magnetic lasso uses Sobel edge detection combined with Dijkstra’s shortest-path algorithm to find the strongest edge path between anchor points. It snaps to high-contrast boundaries in the image, making it much faster than freehand for complex organic shapes.
codex.entry.tool_crop.content:
Trims the canvas to a selected region. Drag the crop box, then press Enter to apply or Escape to cancel.

The context bar offers AR-matching presets — common aspect ratios that snap the crop box to exact proportions. Useful for preparing images for specific output sizes.
codex.entry.tool_transform.content:
Move, scale, and rotate the current layer or selection. Drag handles to resize, drag inside to move. Enter applies, Escape cancels, Shift constrains proportions.

Warp modes (available in context bar when transform is active):

• Affine — move control points freely. Most flexible deformation.

• Similitude — preserves angles (no skew). Scale + rotate only.

• Rigid — preserves distances as much as possible. Minimal distortion, good for repositioning elements naturally.

• Perspective — four-corner pin for perspective correction or deliberate perspective distortion.

All warp modes use Moving Least Squares (MLS) interpolation for smooth, natural-looking deformations. Grid subdivision renders the transformed result at high quality.
codex.entry.tool_transform.deep: The MLS warp implementation follows the Schaefer, McPhail & Warren paper with three deformation types (Affine, Similitude, Rigid). Grid subdivision renders the warp by tessellating the canvas into a grid and transforming each cell, avoiding the visual artifacts that per-pixel sampling would produce. The perspective transform uses standard 4-point homography. Algorithms ported from Krita’s GPL-3.0 source (tool_transform2/), which is license-compatible with Studio’s AGPL-3.0 via Section 13.
codex.entry.tool_radial_symmetry.content:
Studio supports three symmetry modes, toggled from the context bar buttons:

• Horizontal — mirrors strokes left/right across a vertical center line.

• Vertical — mirrors strokes top/bottom across a horizontal center line.

• Radial — replicates strokes around a center point with configurable axis count. Set 2 for simple mirror, 6+ for snowflake/kaleidoscope patterns, 8+ for mandala designs.

Works with brush, eraser, and smudge. In radial mode, each symmetry segment maintains its own independent smudge buffer so smudge strokes don’t bleed between segments.

Combined mode (horizontal + vertical) gives four-way symmetry.
codex.entry.gallery_overview.content:
Gallery is Studio’s image library. It browses every generation you’ve ever made, plus any other folder of images you point it at. New images appear in real time as they’re saved — no refresh button.

Getting started: First launch, Gallery asks you to pick a folder. Choose your Forge output folder (or any folder full of images) and it scans everything in it. You can link more folders later via Link new folder at the bottom of the sidebar.

Layout:

• Left sidebar — folder tree on top, character tag list below. Drag the column divider to resize. Click a folder or tag to filter; click again to clear.

• Top bar — search box and sort controls. Search matches filenames, character tags, and embedded metadata (prompt text, models, LoRAs). Autocomplete suggests known tags and metadata words as you type. Sort by filename, newest, or folder — click a sort button again to flip direction.

• Main grid — your images, infinite-scrolling. Click any image for the detail view.

Character tags: Gallery auto-parses character names out of filenames using common patterns (underscores and dashes connect name parts, commas and plus signs separate characters). A file named John_Doe+Jane_Smith_seed123.png becomes tagged with John Doe and Jane Smith. The tag list in the sidebar groups tags by first letter.

You can manually add or remove tags per image from the detail view. See Gallery Settings in Gallery itself for the full tagging rules and the ignore words list (words that never become tags, like "seed" or "sample").

Detail view: Click any image. You get full resolution, embedded metadata (prompt, settings, seed, LoRAs, sampler, everything stored in the PNG), character tags (editable), filename (editable), and action buttons for send-to-canvas, rename, move, delete, and open-in-explorer. Arrow keys navigate to adjacent images without closing the view.

Real-time updates: The dot in the topbar shows watcher status. Green = connected, gray = disconnected. New generations appear in the grid within a second of being saved. Move, rename, or delete a file outside of Studio and Gallery picks it up too.

Send to Canvas: Load any Gallery image onto your Studio canvas for editing. Also reads the embedded metadata — prompt, seed, and settings get restored so you can iterate from there.
codex.entry.gallery_overview.deep: Built on TrackImage by Moritz, integrated as Studio’s Gallery module. Multi-folder scanning via linked scan folders. Filesystem watcher via watchdog; real-time updates via SSE at /studio/gallery/events. suppress_path() prevents duplicate events on rename/move/delete. SQLite backend with PRAGMA busy_timeout=30000 for lock contention. Autocomplete endpoint at /suggest; metadata extracted from SD/ComfyUI/EXIF headers into an indexed search_text column. Video files get thumbnails via ffmpeg. Perceptual hashes (via imagehash) power the duplicate detector.
codex.entry.gallery_organize.content:
Gallery supports bulk operations on multiple images at once. Everything is undoable.

Selection:

• Click an image — selects just that one.

• Ctrl+Click — toggles individual images in and out of the selection.

• Shift+Click — selects a range between the last-clicked image and the one you shift-click.

• A selection bar appears at the top of the grid showing the count and the bulk-action buttons.

Bulk rename: Pick a base name; Gallery numbers multiple files automatically. Use underscores for multi-word character names and commas or plus signs to separate characters — John_Doe+Jane_Smith produces files that Gallery re-parses as two separate character tags. If a filename collision happens, Gallery offers to auto-increment or overwrite.

Bulk move: Drag selected images onto any folder in the sidebar to move them there. You can also use the Move button in the selection bar to pick a destination from a folder picker. Both are undoable — Ctrl+Z moves everything back.

Bulk delete: Deleted images go to Gallery’s trash — they’re not hard-deleted immediately. Ctrl+Z restores them. Trash is per-Gallery, separate from the OS recycle bin.

Clipboard: Cut or copy a selection, then paste into a different folder — like file manager clipboard semantics, but scoped to Gallery. Cut moves, copy duplicates.

Format conversion: Convert selected images between PNG, JPEG, and WebP. For formats without alpha (JPEG), you pick how transparency is handled — skip transparent files, or flatten to white or black. Option to keep originals or replace them. Option to strip in-file metadata during conversion.

Strip metadata: Removes embedded EXIF / PNG metadata from the files on disk. Generation parameters stored in the Gallery database are preserved — the detail-view metadata panel keeps working. Use this before sharing images publicly if you don’t want your prompt and settings going out with them.

Tag editing (bulk): The Tags button in the selection bar opens a bulk tag editor. Shows tags that are common to all selected images, tags that only some have (with a count like 3/5), and lets you add or remove tags across the whole selection at once.

Undo: Ctrl+Z reverses the last Gallery operation — rename, move, delete, copy. The undo stack persists through the current session.

Context menu: Right-click an image for the same actions (rename, move, delete, strip metadata, send to canvas, open in explorer). Right-click a folder in the sidebar for folder-level actions (rename, unlink — which removes the link without deleting files on disk).
codex.entry.gallery_duplicates.content:
Gallery can find visually similar images across your library — not just exact file duplicates, but images that look the same. Useful when you’ve generated the same concept repeatedly with different seeds, or ended up with near-identical outputs you forgot about.

Running a scan: Open the duplicates view from the Gallery topbar. Gallery computes a perceptual hash for each image and groups ones that hash close together. Scans can take a while on large libraries — perceptual hashing is per-image, not something Gallery caches across sessions yet.

Results view: Duplicates appear as groups. Each group shows the similar files side by side with thumbnails, filenames, and folder paths. A header shows the total — e.g. "12 groups · 38 files (keeping one per group would free 26 files)".

Review and delete:

• Each file has a checkbox. Check the ones you want to delete.

• Each group has a Select all but first button — fastest way to keep one copy per group and trash the rest.

• Deletions go to Gallery trash, undoable with Ctrl+Z. If you decide you wanted one back, undo restores it.

What counts as "similar": Perceptual hashing is tolerant of minor differences — small crops, compression, slight color shifts — so cropped and re-saved versions of the same image still cluster. It’s not semantic similarity — two different generations of "a red cat" won’t group unless they came out genuinely close-looking.

Heads up: Duplicate detection requires the imagehash Python package. Studio installs it automatically on first launch, but if Forge failed to install it (offline install, pip issues), this view will tell you it’s unavailable. Manual fix: pip install imagehash inside Forge’s Python environment.
codex.entry.workshop_overview.content:
Workshop is Studio’s model crafting suite. It lets you build new checkpoints by combining existing ones, bake LoRAs and VAEs into checkpoints permanently, and chain multiple operations together in a single recipe.

What you can do:

• Merge two or three checkpoints using any of a dozen methods (Weighted Sum, SLERP, Add Difference, TIES, DARE, STAR, SVD, Cosine Adaptive, Model Stock, and more).

• Bake LoRAs directly into a checkpoint so users don’t need to load them separately.

• Bake VAEs into a checkpoint so users don’t need to pick one.

• Chain multiple operations as a recipe — merge A and B, bake a LoRA into the result, swap the VAE, save. One recipe, one output.

Two modes per operation:

• Test Merge — hot-swaps the new weights into memory without writing a file. Generate test images to judge the result before committing.

• Save to Disk — writes the final checkpoint as .safetensors.

Every completed merge or bake auto-logs to the Journal (see the entry on that below) with its full recipe, so you can reproduce any experiment later.
codex.entry.workshop_methods.content:
Workshop offers a menu of merge methods, each with its own strengths. Pick based on what you’re trying to achieve, not by instinct — the differences are real.

Basic blending:

• Weighted Sum — linear interpolation between A and B per alpha. Simplest, fastest, always works. Start here.

• SLERP — spherical interpolation. Preserves the magnitude of weight vectors instead of averaging them. Usually produces sharper, more vibrant results than Weighted Sum at the same alpha. Best when blending two models of similar quality.

Training transplant:

• Add Difference — extracts what B learned relative to its base model C, then applies that training to A. Use this to transplant a finetune’s skills (e.g. anime style, specific subject) into a different model. Requires Model C — the base B was finetuned from.

Noise-filtered merges (for messy finetunes):

• TIES — Trim, Elect Sign & Merge. Extracts B’s training over A, trims the weakest changes, keeps only significant ones. Cleaner than Weighted Sum when B is overtrained.

• DARE — randomly drops a fraction of B’s training delta, rescales the rest. Same goal as TIES with a different noise model.

• DELLA — like DARE but drop probability is magnitude-weighted, so large important changes survive while small noisy ones are more likely to be removed. Slightly more reliable than uniform DARE.

• DARE-TIES — combines both.

Spectral methods:

• STAR (Spectral) — Spectral Truncation and Rescale. Decomposes B’s training via SVD and strips noisy spectral components. Produces cleaner results from overtrained or messy finetunes. Safe range: lambda 0.3–0.5 with eta 0.1. Lambda 1.0 corrupts output.

• SVD: Structure A + Mag B / Structure B + Mag A — decomposes both via SVD and recombines. "Structure" is what a layer detects; "magnitude" is how strongly it responds. Swap them independently — results are impossible via weight averaging.

• SVD: Spectral Blend — Procrustes-aligns both models’ spectral decompositions, then interpolates structure and magnitude together in spectral space. Smoother than Weighted Sum because it respects geometric relationships between feature directions.

Adaptive:

• Cosine Adaptive — computes per-tensor cosine similarity between A and B, uses similar tensors for higher blend weight (they agree, safe to merge) and dissimilar ones for lower weight (they disagree, merging would average useful signal into noise).

• Model Stock — no alpha required. Computes the optimal blend automatically from the models themselves.

When in doubt: Weighted Sum 0.5 is always a reasonable baseline. If results look washed out, try SLERP next. If you’re merging a specific finetune of a known base, use Add Difference. If one of the inputs is visibly overtrained, use STAR or TIES.
codex.entry.workshop_block_weights.conte:
Instead of merging every layer of the model at the same alpha, block weights let you assign different alphas to different parts of the UNet. This gives you much finer control over what gets merged where.

Block structure (SDXL):

• Input blocks — early layers process the noisy latent. Later input blocks handle structural features, composition, and layout.

• Middle — the bottleneck. Most compressed representation of the image, the model’s core understanding. Affects global coherence.

• Output blocks — expand back to full resolution. Early output blocks = structure, later = fine details and rendering quality.

Common patterns:

• Keep A’s composition, use B’s rendering — low alpha on input and middle blocks, high alpha on late output blocks.

• Keep A’s style, use B’s subject knowledge — opposite: high alpha on input/middle, low on output.

• Transplant faces only — very localized block weights on the blocks that tend to encode face features.

Block weights work with Weighted Sum, SLERP, Add Difference, STAR, and all SVD methods. TIES, DARE, and DELLA operate on the whole model at once and ignore block weights.
codex.entry.workshop_lora_bake.content:
LoRA baking permanently merges a LoRA (or several) into a checkpoint. The result is a single .safetensors file that behaves as if the LoRA is always active at the baked strength — no <lora:...> tags needed.

When to bake instead of loading as a LoRA:

• You always use this LoRA with this checkpoint.

• You want to distribute the result as a self-contained model.

• You’re stacking multiple LoRAs and want a simpler runtime setup.

• You want the LoRA’s effect at a weight not expressible at load time (e.g. bake at 0.7 plus another at 0.4 for a specific blend).

Supported adapter types: LoRA, LoHa, LoKr, OFT, BOFT, GLoRA, DoRA, plus raw diff and set patches. Studio auto-detects the adapter type per file from its key suffixes — you don’t have to tell it what kind of LoRA you’re baking.

Tips:

• Bake at a strength you’ve tested first. There’s no slider at runtime after baking — you’re locked in.

• Architecture must match. An SDXL LoRA won’t bake into an SD 1.5 checkpoint.

• Multiple LoRAs in a recipe are applied sequentially, so order can matter for non-linear adapter types.
codex.entry.workshop_lora_bake.deep: All math performed in fp32 to avoid precision loss, downcast only at save. VAE keys always stay fp32 regardless of checkpoint format. Key matching uses four strategies: the standard Kohya lora_unet_*/lora_te*_* prefix convention, Flux/SD3 direct format, generic base.weight matching, and a full CompVis↔diffusers prefix table for SDXL diffusers-format LoRAs. DoRA decomposition is detected via dora_scale keys and applied via the standard weight-norm rescaling after the delta is added.
codex.entry.workshop_vae_bake.content:
VAE baking replaces the VAE embedded in a checkpoint with a different one. The result is a checkpoint that always uses the new VAE — users don’t have to pick one from the dropdown.

When to use it:

• Your favorite checkpoint has baked-in color/saturation issues and a specific VAE fixes them (e.g. vae-ft-mse-840000 on many SD 1.5 checkpoints).

• You’re distributing a checkpoint and want it to "just work" without VAE setup.

• You’re building a self-contained recipe with merges, LoRA bakes, and a specific VAE all rolled into one file.

VAE architecture must match checkpoint architecture. SDXL VAE into SD 1.5 checkpoint won’t work. WAN 5B and WAN 2.1 use different VAEs — don’t cross them.
codex.entry.workshop_journal.content:
Every completed merge, LoRA bake, VAE bake, and chain run auto-logs to the Journal with its full recipe. You get a searchable history of every experiment you’ve ever run.

Per-entry:

• Full recipe (method, alpha, models involved, block weights, parameters).

• Timestamp and elapsed time.

• Editable rating (0–5 stars), tags, and notes.

• Optional sample image — attach a test generation to remember what this merge actually looked like.

Search & filter: by text (searches name, notes, and recipe contents), by tag, or by type (merge / LoRA bake / VAE bake / note). You can also add manual notes for models you didn’t make in Workshop but want to track.

Recipes are saved chain configurations — a sequence of merge/bake steps that reference previous step outputs as __O1__, __O2__, etc. Save a recipe once, run it against different input models any time. Intermediate outputs are auto-deleted unless you check Save intermediates.
codex.entry.canvas_routing.content:
Studio doesn’t have separate txt2img and img2img tabs. You paint on the canvas, hit Generate, and Studio checks the pixels to decide the routing:

• Canvas is blank → txt2img (generates from scratch)

• Canvas has content → img2img (works with what’s there)

• Mask painted → inpainting (changes only the masked area)

You never pick a mode. The canvas state is the mode. If you sketch something, erase it all, and generate — that’s txt2img, because the canvas is blank. The pixels are the source of truth, not a mode toggle.

Zoom behavior: At 200%+ zoom, pixels render crisp (nearest-neighbor) instead of blurry. Below 200%, standard bilinear smoothing applies. This matches Krita’s behavior and makes pixel-level editing clean.
codex.entry.canvas_routing.deep: isCanvasBlank() checks actual pixel data on every generation call. It’s the sole source of truth for routing — no dirty flags or mode toggles involved. A completely transparent canvas routes to txt2img. Any non-transparent pixel routes to img2img. Mask presence is checked separately and takes priority.
codex.entry.documents.content:
Studio is a multi-document editor. The thin tab strip across the top of the canvas area lets you keep several separate workspaces open at once, like tabs in a browser or Photoshop.

Each document is fully independent. When you switch tabs, Studio swaps the entire workspace:

• Layers, mask, selection, and regional prompt boxes

• Undo/redo stack

• Zoom level and pan position

• The entire Generate panel — prompt, negative, seed, sampler, CFG, steps, denoise, dimensions, batch, HiRes Fix settings, all 3 ADetailer slots, Soft Inpainting, inpaint area/fill/blur/padding

So Doc 1 can be a portrait you’re refining at 768×1024 with one prompt and seed, while Doc 2 is a landscape experiment at 1216×832 with a completely different prompt. Switching between them is instant — nothing bleeds across.

Working with tabs:

• + button at the end of the strip — creates a new blank document.

• Click a tab to switch to it.

• × on hover — closes that document. You can’t close the last one. Closing a document with unsaved canvas changes prompts for confirmation.

• Double-click a tab name to rename inline. Enter to commit, Escape to cancel.

• Ctrl+] and Ctrl+[ cycle forward and backward through documents.

• A small dot (•) appears next to a tab’s name when it has unsaved changes since the last snapshot.

Show Me tutorials use this system. When you click a Show Me button in Codex, Studio opens the tutorial in a new document so your real work stays untouched. When the tutorial ends, the tutorial document is cleaned up automatically.
codex.entry.documents.deep: Implemented in studio-docs.js. Each document holds a full snapshot: layers[], activeLayerIdx, mask, regions, undoStack/redoStack, zoom, panX/panY, W/H, plus the Generate panel state (values, selects, textareas, checkboxes — see _GEN_VALUES/_GEN_SELECTS/_GEN_TEXTAREAS/_GEN_CHECKS in the source). _saveDoc() snapshots to the current index; _loadDoc() restores a target. Switching always saves the outgoing doc first so no work is lost. Uses a multi-page pattern — the live canvas state is a window into the active document, not a separate store.
codex.entry.scrub_labels.content:
The context bar values (Size, Opacity, Hardness, Smoothing, Strength) aren’t traditional sliders — they’re scrub labels:

• Drag horizontally on the label to adjust the value (like Photoshop’s scrubby sliders).

• Click the label to open a text input and type an exact number.

Sensitivity scales to the value range — fine controls (like hardness 0–1) scrub slower than coarse ones (like size 1–100). You’ll get used to these fast.
codex.entry.per_tool_memory.content:
Each brush-type tool (brush, eraser, smudge, blur, dodge, clone, liquify, shape) remembers its own settings independently. Size, opacity, hardness, smoothing, and strength are stored per-tool.

Switch from brush to eraser and back — your brush settings are exactly where you left them. These persist across sessions via local storage.
codex.entry.shift_drag_resize.content:
Hold Shift and drag on the canvas: horizontal movement adjusts brush size, vertical movement adjusts opacity. Krita-style.

Works for brush, eraser, smudge, blur, dodge, clone, and liquify. Much faster than reaching for the context bar or using bracket keys.
codex.entry.drag_and_drop.content:
Drop an image file onto the canvas and Studio:

1. Resizes the canvas to match the image dimensions.

2. Creates a new layer with the image.

3. Updates width/height inputs and AR indicator.

4. Reads PNG metadata and imports generation parameters if found (prompt, seed, settings).

5. Disables Hires Fix automatically (since you’re loading an existing image).

This is the fastest way to bring an external image into Studio for editing or img2img.
codex.entry.send_to_canvas.content:
Sending an output to the canvas (button, right-click, or drag from the output strip) creates a new layer — it doesn’t overwrite your work.

Pipeline results (generation, upscale) go to a reusable "Gen Result" layer. Gallery sends also create new layers. Your painted edits on other layers are always safe.
codex.entry.ar_buttons.content:
The AR buttons have interactive behavior beyond simple presets:

• Click a ratio → applies it and resizes the canvas.

• Re-click the active ratio → swaps portrait/landscape (e.g. 3:2 becomes 2:3).

• Both actions are undoable — Ctrl+Z restores the previous canvas and dimensions.

When AR Randomization is enabled, the buttons switch to pool-toggle mode — click to include/exclude ratios from the random pool instead of applying them directly. Same behavior for base resolution buttons.
codex.entry.extension_bridge.content:
Studio doesn’t ignore Forge’s extension ecosystem — it wraps it. When you install a compatible Forge extension (anything that registers as an AlwaysOn script), Studio auto-detects it and surfaces its controls in the Extensions panel alongside ControlNet and ADetailer.

How it works: On startup Studio scans Forge’s script runner for extensions, reads their control definitions, and builds UI for each one. Checkboxes, sliders, dropdowns, and dependent visibility all work automatically. No per-extension code required.

You can toggle any bridged extension on or off in Settings. Disabled extensions never run even if the original Forge UI would have had them active.

What Studio handles natively instead: ControlNet, ADetailer, Soft Inpainting, Dynamic Prompts (wildcards), and AR Selector are replaced with Studio’s own native UI rather than bridged — they’re more deeply integrated.
codex.entry.extension_bridge.deep: Two-tier discovery: Blueprint Bridge reads an optional blueprint.json next to an extension’s script (or bundled under blueprints/) for an explicit layout override and optional hooks module. AutoBridge falls back to reflective layout analysis — walks the Gradio component tree, detects Column/Group containers as group IDs, and probes .change callbacks to infer visibility dependencies and value propagation. The /studio/extensions manifest endpoint returns controls, groups, layout, dependencies, and any blueprint metadata. Tier 1 native modules are hardcoded into a NATIVE_TITLES exclusion set.
codex.entry.wildcards_editor.content:
The Wildcards tab in the main tab bar is a full editor for your wildcard files. The Wildcards entry under Generation covers what wildcards are and the __name__ syntax — this entry is about the editor itself.

Layout: File tree on the left, editor on the right. Click any file in the tree to open it. The dot next to the filename turns amber when there are unsaved changes.

Creating files and folders: Use + File and + Folder at the top of the tree. New files open immediately for editing. Folders nest — a file at characters/hair.txt is referenced as __characters/hair__ in your prompts.

Right-click a file or folder for rename, duplicate, move, and delete. You can also drag files between folders directly in the tree.

Search modes: The search box has two modes, toggled with the Aa button next to it.

• Filename mode (default) — filters the tree to files matching the query.

• Content mode — searches inside files. Useful when you remember a phrase you wrote but not which file it’s in.

Live preview: Open the preview pane to see resolved sample lines from your wildcard file — including nested wildcards if your file references other wildcards. Refreshes as you edit.

Auto-save: Toggle on to save changes ~1.5 seconds after you stop typing. Off by default — use Ctrl+S manually if you prefer.

Import / Export: Export your full wildcards collection as a .zip for backup or sharing. Import drops a .zip back into your wildcards folder — existing files with the same name are skipped, not overwritten.
codex.entry.session_vs_defaults.content:
Studio has two separate ways to make your settings persist between launches. They overlap in confusing ways, so here’s the difference.

Workflow Defaults — a saved baseline you set deliberately. Settings → Defaults → Save Current as Defaults snapshots your current parameters (sampler, CFG, steps, dimensions, AD slots, etc.) and saves them server-side. Every launch starts from this baseline. Use Reset to clear them and go back to factory settings.

Think of this as: "this is how I want Studio to start every time."

Remember Last Session — picks up where you left off. Toggle on in Settings → Generation. Your prompts and settings save automatically when you close the page and restore on the next launch. Stored in your browser, not on the server.

Think of this as: "don’t make me retype my prompt every time I refresh."

Picking which categories Session Memory saves: When the toggle is on, an accordion appears with checkboxes for each category. Uncheck what you don’t want carried over.

• Prompts — positive and negative prompt text.

• Generation params & AR pools — sampler, scheduler, CFG, steps, dimensions, batch settings, AR randomization pools.

• Hires Fix — enabled state, upscaler, scale, denoise, etc.

• ADetailer — all 3 slots’ enable state, model, prompts, confidence, denoise.

• Upscale — upscaler model, scale, post-AD toggle.

• Canvas settings — grid, pen pressure, save outputs, live preview, metadata, panel collapse states.

• Brush settings — brush size.

• Output format — PNG/JPEG/WebP and quality.

• Inpaint settings — area mode, fill, mask blur, padding.

Which order they apply: If both are active on launch, Session Memory wins — it loads last, on top of Defaults. Categories you unchecked in Session fall back to whatever Defaults have.

Quick guide:

• Want to always start with the same baseline? Defaults.

• Want continuity between sessions? Session Memory.

• Want both? Set Defaults to your ideal baseline, turn Session Memory on, uncheck the categories you want Defaults to win for.

Session Memory feature suggested by Railer.
codex.entry.param_prompt.content:
Describe what you want to see in plain language. "A red fox in a snowy forest" works. So does "portrait of a woman with gold earrings, oil painting style, cinematic lighting." More specific prompts give more controlled results.

Structure: A good pattern is subject first, then style, then quality. For example: "a knight standing on a cliff overlooking a valley, fantasy art, dramatic lighting, highly detailed." The AI pays the most attention to words near the beginning, so front-load what matters most.

Emphasis: Control word strength with parentheses:

(important word:1.3) — 30% stronger. (less important:0.7) — 30% weaker. Plain (word) defaults to 1.1. Nesting stacks: ((word)) = 1.21. Stay in the 0.5–1.5 range — extreme values cause artifacts. Use sparingly; overweighting everything defeats the purpose.

BREAK: Forces a chunk boundary between sections. Useful for separating concepts that interfere with each other. A structured approach:

style, lighting, composition BREAK subject count BREAK detailed description, pose, background

Establishing "how it looks" before "what it is" helps consistent stylistic choices. Simple prompts don’t need BREAK — it shines with complex, detailed generations.

Token counter: The counter below the prompt shows token usage. The AI processes text in ~75-token chunks. Going past one chunk is fine, but words in later chunks may have less influence.

Patterns that work well:

• Quality tags: "masterpiece, best quality, highly detailed"

• Lighting: "soft natural light," "dramatic rim lighting," "golden hour"

• Camera: "close-up portrait," "wide shot," "low angle," "85mm lens"

• Art styles: "watercolor," "oil painting," "anime style," "digital painting"

• Specificity over vagueness: "burgundy velvet dress" beats "nice dress"

Prompt troubleshooting:

• Wrong number of subjects: Reinforce with solo. Move count tags earlier. Add multiple girls to negatives.

• Style not coming through: Front-load style tags. Use multiple related descriptors.

• Background overwhelming subject: Add blurred background, bokeh, depth of field. Move subject earlier.

• Concepts bleeding together: Add more BREAKs. Try prompt scheduling for staged introduction.
codex.entry.param_prompt.deep:
Advanced syntax:

Prompt Scheduling [from:to:when] — transitions between concepts during generation. when is a decimal (fraction of steps) or integer (exact step). Example: [sketch:finished rendering:0.5] starts sketch-like, transitions to polished at 50%. Shorthand: [to:when] adds "to" after a step; [from::when] removes "from" after a step. Does NOT work with LoRA or Hypernetworks.

Prompt Alternating [concept|concept] — alternates each step, creating blended effects. [red hair|blonde hair] produces strawberry blonde. [rain|snow] creates mixed precipitation. Does NOT work with LoRA or Hypernetworks.

Composable Diffusion AND — processes prompts separately and combines results. a portrait of a woman AND cyberpunk style. Supports per-section weights: a cat :1.2 AND a dog AND a penguin :2.2. Commas = single combined concept; AND = separate processing then merge. Can cause "overbaked" results at high CFG — lower to ~5.

Escaping — literal brackets with backslash: anime_\(character\). Comments after # are ignored by some extensions.

Extra Networks — <lora:filename:multiplier> and <hypernet:filename:multiplier> are processed and removed from the prompt before generation. Cannot be used in negative prompts or with scheduling/alternating.

Token counter shows per-encoder counts (CLIP-L and CLIP-G for SDXL) and chunk count. Multiple chunks work via prompt chunk concatenation — later chunks have full attention within themselves but reduced cross-chunk influence. Wildcard syntax __wildcard_name__ resolved before encoding via dynamicprompts.
codex.entry.param_neg.content:
Tells the AI what to avoid. Common negatives: "blurry, ugly, deformed, extra fingers, bad anatomy, watermark, text."

A few key words is usually enough — long negative prompts have diminishing returns. Only negate things you genuinely don’t want. If you say "red" in positive and "red" in negative, they fight each other and you get muddy results.

Wildcards like __neg4__ can insert pre-made negative templates if you have wildcard files installed.
codex.entry.param_neg.deep: CFG negative conditioning. Same syntax as positive (emphasis, BREAK, wildcards). Applied as the unconditional direction the model steers away from during each denoising step. Overly aggressive negatives reduce image quality — the model spends guidance budget avoiding things rather than building what you asked for.
codex.entry.param_cfg.content:
Controls how closely the AI follows your prompt. Higher values = more literal but can oversaturate. Lower values = more creative freedom.

Recommended ranges by architecture:

• SD 1.5: 7–11

• SDXL / Illustrious: 5–7

• Flux: 3.5–5

• Distilled models: 1.0 (guidance is baked into the distillation)

If your images look artificial, oversaturated, or have harsh colors, try lowering CFG first — it’s the most common fix.
codex.entry.param_steps.content:
How many refinement passes the AI makes. More steps = more detail but takes longer.

Sweet spot: 20–30. Use 15–20 when experimenting, 28–35 for polished finals. Above 40 rarely helps with most samplers.
codex.entry.param_steps.deep: Diminishing returns above ~30 for most samplers. DPM++ 2M SDE and Euler converge faster than ancestral samplers. Ancestral samplers (anything with "a" in the name) never fully converge — results change with every step count increment.
codex.entry.param_denoise.content:
Controls how much the AI changes what’s already on the canvas. Only matters when the canvas has content (img2img mode).

• 1.0 — full replace. Only prompt matters.

• 0.7–0.8 — major changes. Good for rough sketches.

• 0.4–0.6 — moderate. Adjusts while keeping structure.

• 0.2–0.3 — gentle. Refines details without altering composition.

Start high and lower it as you get closer to what you want.
codex.entry.param_denoise.deep: Denoise maps to how many steps actually execute: 0.7 at 30 steps = 21 actual denoising steps. Auto-routing: blank+pristine canvas → txt2img, canvas with content → img2img, blank+dirty (erased) canvas → img2img.
codex.entry.param_sampler.content:
The sampler controls the denoising algorithm. The scheduler controls how noise decreases across steps.

Recommended starting points:

• SD 1.5 / SDXL / Illustrious: Euler a or DPM++ 2M SDE + Karras

• Flux: Euler

• Distilled models: Euler (4–8 steps)

Don’t stress about this early on — the difference is often subtle between samplers at the same step count.

Ancestral vs non-ancestral: Samplers with "a" in the name (Euler a, DPM++ 2S a) are ancestral — they add randomness each step, producing more variety but never converging to a stable result. Non-ancestral samplers (DPM++ 2M, Euler) converge asymptotically — results stabilize as step count increases.
codex.entry.param_sampler.deep: All Forge samplers available including bundled custom samplers: BFS (Bandwise Flow), Cacophony, Labyrinth, Parasite, Parasitic BFS, Grimoire, Adams-Bashforth. Custom "Toxic" scheduler uses a φ³ power curve with progressive Karras blending.
codex.entry.param_dimensions.content:
Base buttons (512–1024) set the overall resolution. Ratio buttons set the shape (square, wide, tall). The ⇄ button swaps orientation.

Recommended resolutions:

• SDXL / Illustrious: 1024×1024 square. For portrait: 832×1216. For landscape: 1216×832.

• SD 1.5: 512×512 or 768×768. Non-square: 512×768, 768×512.

• Flux: 1024×1024 standard.

Going far below or above the model’s native resolution without HiRes Fix can produce artifacts — duplicate limbs, distorted compositions. Use HiRes Fix for large, detailed output rather than generating at high resolution directly.

Bigger images take more VRAM and longer to generate.
codex.entry.param_seed_batch.content:
Seed: -1 = random (dice button). Same seed + same settings = same image. Click the recycle button to lock the seed and make variations by tweaking prompts or other settings.

Extra → Variation Seed lets you blend between two seeds for subtle variations.

Batch Count: generates multiple images sequentially, each with a different seed. Batch Size: generates multiple simultaneously (uses more VRAM).
codex.entry.hires_fix.content:
Two-pass generation: generates at normal size first, then upscales and adds detail. Produces much sharper results than generating at large resolution directly.

Scale: enlargement factor (2.0 = double dimensions).

Denoise: 0.2–0.4 for detail without changing content. Higher values reshape more.

Steps: 0 = same as first pass.

Upscaler: "Latent" = smooth/painterly feel. R-ESRGAN = sharp/detailed.
codex.entry.hires_fix.deep: Base resolution → latent or pixel upscale → img2img at scaled resolution. Separate CFG and checkpoint overrides available for the second pass. Output dimensions = base × scale; UI restores base dims after generation. HiRes + ADetailer = multiplicative cost — each adds a full pass.
codex.entry.loras.content:
What are LoRAs? Small add-on files that teach an existing model new things — a specific art style, a character’s face, a type of clothing. They’re much smaller than full models (typically 10–200 MB vs 2–7 GB) and you can stack multiple at once.

Finding LoRAs: Browse CivitAI and filter by "LoRA" type. Check the architecture matches your model (SD1.5 or SDXL). Read the description for trigger words — many LoRAs require a specific word in your prompt to activate.

Installing: Download the .safetensors file, put it in models/Lora/. Available immediately, no restart needed.

Using: Add <lora:filename:weight> to your prompt. Or click the LORA button near the prompt box to browse installed LoRAs and insert them by clicking.

Weight guide: 0.3–0.5 = subtle influence. 0.6–0.8 = typical sweet spot. 0.9–1.0 = full strength (can artifact if overtrained). Above 1.0 = risky. When stacking multiple LoRAs, lower individual weights to prevent conflicts.

Common problems: No effect = wrong architecture or missing trigger word. Burnt/oversaturated = weight too high. Same face every time = overtrained, lower to 0.3–0.5.
codex.entry.loras.deep: Standard A1111 syntax. Applied at generation time via Forge’s LoRA loading system — modifies specific weight matrices in UNet and/or text encoder. The LoRA browser reads sidecar .json metadata files. Cards show trigger words, search matches against them, and clicking auto-inserts the tag and trigger words at the preferred weight.
codex.entry.wildcards.content:
Wildcards insert randomized text into your prompts. Write __wildcard_name__ and Studio replaces it with a random line from a matching text file. Every generation picks a different line, giving you automatic variety.

Creating a wildcard file:

1. Go to your wildcards folder: extensions/sd-dynamic-prompts/wildcards/

2. Create a plain text file. The filename becomes the wildcard name (colors.txt → __colors__).

3. Put one option per line.

4. Use it in your prompt: a __colors__ dress in a garden

Folders: Organize wildcards into folders. A file at wildcards/characters/hair.txt is used as __characters/hair__.

Management: The WILDCARD button near the prompt box opens the wildcard browser. The Wildcards tab in the main tab bar provides a full editor with folder tree, file creation, editing, and preview.
codex.entry.live_painting.content:
Paint on the canvas and watch the AI regenerate after every stroke. Studio treats your canvas as an img2img input and re-runs generation every time you stop painting.

How to use it:

1. Click the ▶ Live button next to the Generate button.

2. Paint on the canvas. Each time you release the pointer, Studio submits the current canvas and prompt and generates a result.

3. The result appears in the preview pane in the bottom-right of the viewport — not as a canvas layer. This keeps your paint work untouched while you compare.

4. Click Apply to commit the current preview to a new [Live] layer below your active layer. After apply, the seed re-rolls automatically so your next iteration explores a new variation.

5. Click ■ Live again to stop.

Controls while Live is active:

• Seed lock — Live keeps the same seed across iterations so small canvas changes produce small, coherent result changes. Reroll it manually with the seed button if you want a fresh variation.

• Strength — the img2img denoise applied each iteration. Lower (0.2–0.4) = the AI respects your paint closely. Higher (0.5–0.7) = the AI takes more creative liberty. Adjust mid-session; Live re-submits on change.

• Prompt changes also trigger re-submission, with a short debounce so you can finish typing.

Typical workflow: rough-paint a composition → Live cleans it up into an image → tweak the paint, let Live re-run → Apply when you like a result → paint over the applied layer to direct the next pass. This lets you steer generations by hand instead of by prompt.

Things to know:

• ADetailer auto-disables while Live is active — it’s too expensive to run every iteration. It restores when you stop Live.

• Live submits on pointer-up (end of stroke), not continuously. Long strokes don’t stack up multiple generations.

• Each iteration is a full diffusion run, so keep steps modest (10–15 is plenty for Live) and resolution reasonable (768×768 is a good default).
codex.entry.live_painting.deep: Completion-triggered img2img loop adapted from Krita AI Diffusion’s approach. Frontend handles change detection (pointer-up hash diff), debounce (300ms canvas, 500ms prompt), and an adaptive watchdog timeout (2× rolling average generation time, clamped 10–30s). Backend queue in studio_live.py drops stale requests when a new one arrives mid-generation. Preview compositing happens in canvas-core.js via setLivePreview() — results display in the existing viewport preview pane rather than as a canvas layer, so the preview can be discarded without touching user paint. applyLivePreview() creates the [Live] layer below the active layer so subsequent paint lands on top.
codex.entry.adetailer.content:
Automatically detects faces (and optionally hands/bodies) in your generated image and regenerates them at higher detail. Fixes the most common AI problem — faces looking wrong or blurry at smaller sizes.

3 slots available. Slot 1 is pre-configured for face detection. Enable the checkbox and it runs automatically after generation.

Each slot has its own prompt (describe the face specifically), confidence (how sure the detector must be), and denoise (how much to change).

When used with regional prompting, each region’s per-character prompt is automatically routed to the face inpainting step — the model fixing a specific character’s face sees only that character’s description, not the whole scene prompt.
codex.entry.adetailer.deep: Post-generation YOLO-based detection + inpainting pipeline. Per-slot flow: detect → crop region → inpaint at higher resolution → composite back. Models: face_yolov8n, hand_yolov8n, etc. Runs after HiRes Fix if both enabled. Studio’s fork adds centroid-based region filtering so each detected face is matched to the region that contains its centroid, then inpainted with ad_prompt (character-only, post-wildcard-resolution text). An is_hr_pass discriminator distinguishes hires denoise from AD inner passes so attention couple stays active for hires but steps aside for single-face AD.
